stem: A function \( f \) is sampled at five equally spaced points with spacing \( h = [[h]] \): \( f(0) = [[y0]] \), \( f([[x1]]) = [[y1]] \), \( f([[x2]]) = [[y2]] \), \( f([[x3]]) = [[y3]] \), \( f([[x4]]) = [[y4]] \). Using the trapezoidal rule with these four subintervals, approximate \( \displaystyle\int_{0}^{[[x4]]} f(x)\,dx \).
solution: The trapezoidal rule with spacing \( h \) gives \( \dfrac{h}{2}\big(y_0 + 2y_1 + 2y_2 + 2y_3 + y_4\big) = \dfrac{[[h]]}{2}\big([[y0]] + 2([[y1]]) + 2([[y2]]) + 2([[y3]]) + [[y4]]\big) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to double the interior ordinates \( y_1, y_2, y_3 \).
This omits the factor of \( \tfrac{1}{2} \) in \( \tfrac{h}{2} \).
This both omits the \( \tfrac{1}{2} \) and fails to double the interior ordinates.
This applies Simpson's weights \( (1,4,2,4,1)\tfrac{h}{3} \) instead of the trapezoidal rule.
This doubles the endpoints instead of the interior ordinates.
This is off by one from the correct approximation.

stem: A function \( f \) is sampled at five equally spaced points with spacing \( h = [[h]] \): \( f(0) = [[y0]] \), \( f([[x1]]) = [[y1]] \), \( f([[x2]]) = [[y2]] \), \( f([[x3]]) = [[y3]] \), \( f([[x4]]) = [[y4]] \). Using Simpson's rule (with these four subintervals grouped into two double-panels), approximate \( \displaystyle\int_{0}^{[[x4]]} f(x)\,dx \).
solution: Simpson's rule with spacing \( h \) gives \( \dfrac{h}{3}\big(y_0 + 4y_1 + 2y_2 + 4y_3 + y_4\big) = \dfrac{[[h]]}{3}\big([[y0]] + 4([[y1]]) + 2([[y2]]) + 4([[y3]]) + [[y4]]\big) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This applies the trapezoidal rule's weights instead of Simpson's.
This swaps the \( 4 \) and \( 2 \) weights on the interior points.
This omits all the Simpson weights, just averaging with \( \tfrac{h}{3} \).
This omits the \( \tfrac{1}{3} \) factor entirely.
This shifts the alternating weight pattern by one position.
This is off by one from the correct approximation.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[A]]x^{2} + ([[B]])x + ([[C]]) \). Using the composite trapezoidal rule with 4 equal subintervals of width \( h = [[h]] \) on \( [[[a]], [[b]]] \), approximate \( \displaystyle\int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} f(x)\,dx \).
solution: Evaluating \( f \) at the five equally spaced nodes and combining with trapezoidal weights \( \dfrac{h}{2}(1,2,2,2,1) \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets both the \( \tfrac{1}{2} \) factor and the doubling of interior nodes.
This applies Simpson's weights instead of the trapezoidal rule's.
This reports the EXACT integral of \( f \), not the trapezoidal approximation.
This forgets to double the interior node values.
This is off by one from the correct approximation.
This is off by one from the correct approximation.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[A]]x^{3} + [[B]]x^{2} + ([[C]])x + ([[D]]) \). Using Simpson's rule with the midpoint \( m = [[m]] \) (so two subintervals of width \( [[k]] \)) on \( [[[a]], [[b]]] \), approximate \( \displaystyle\int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} f(x)\,dx \).
solution: Evaluating \( f \) at \( a, m, b \) and combining with Simpson's weights \( \dfrac{k}{3}(1,4,1) \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This applies the trapezoidal rule's weights instead of Simpson's.
This forgets the \( \tfrac{1}{3} \) factor entirely.
This forgets to weight the midpoint value by \( 4 \).
This reports the EXACT integral of \( f \), not Simpson's approximation.
This is off by one from the correct approximation.
This is off by one from the correct approximation.

stem: A function \( f \) is sampled at four UNEVENLY spaced points: \( f([[x0]]) = [[y0]] \), \( f([[x1]]) = [[y1]] \), \( f([[x2]]) = [[y2]] \), \( f([[x3]]) = [[y3]] \), where the subinterval widths are \( x_1-x_0 = [[h1]] \), \( x_2-x_1 = [[h2]] \), \( x_3-x_2 = [[h3]] \). Using the trapezoidal rule on each subinterval, approximate \( \displaystyle\int_{[[x0]]}^{[[x3]]} f(x)\,dx \).
solution: Summing the trapezoidal area of each (possibly different-width) panel gives \( \dfrac{h_1}{2}(y_0+y_1) + \dfrac{h_2}{2}(y_1+y_2) + \dfrac{h_3}{2}(y_2+y_3) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes uniform spacing, using the average width for every panel.
This forgets the \( \tfrac{1}{2} \) factor on each panel.
This drops the last panel entirely.
This uses the subinterval widths in the wrong order.
This is off by one from the correct approximation.
This is off by one from the correct approximation.

stem: A function \( f \) is sampled at four equally spaced points with spacing \( h = [[h]] \): \( f(x_0) = [[y0]] \), \( f(x_1) = [[y1]] \), \( f(x_2) = [[y2]] \), and \( f(x_3) = y_3 \) (unknown). The trapezoidal rule estimate of \( \int_{x_0}^{x_3} f(x)\,dx \) using these four points is \( [[T]] \). What is \( y_3 \)?
solution: From \( T = \dfrac{h}{2}(y_0+2y_1+2y_2+y_3) \), solving for \( y_3 \) gives \( y_3 = \dfrac{2T}{h} - y_0 - 2y_1 - 2y_2 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to double \( y_1 \) and \( y_2 \) when inverting the formula.
This forgets the factor of \( 2 \) multiplying \( T/h \).
This has the sign of the known terms flipped.
This ignores the spacing \( h \) and the required doubling of interior terms entirely.
This is off by one from the correct missing value.
This is off by one from the correct missing value.
